description:
You are working as a freelance penetration tester.  
    The client has supplied you with a single INI-formatted inventory that lists every Internet-facing service in their small office network.  
    Your goal is to parse this INI file, evaluate each advertised service version against the vulnerability policy shown below, and then write two artefacts that the security team will later ingest automatically.  
    All verification of your work will be carried out by an automated grader, so **pay very close attention to the exact file paths, names, and output formats that are required**.  
    You do NOT need root privileges to finish the task.

    1. Source data  
       • Location: /home/user/scan_data/vulnerable-services.ini  
       • Format: Standard INI. Each section name has the form “<ip>-<service>”.  
         Inside every section the following keys exist exactly once and all values are single-line:  
           port   – the TCP port number (integer)  
           service – service name in lowercase (string)  
           version – full upstream version string (string)  

    2. Vulnerability policy you must apply  
       The client’s policy states a service is *vulnerable* when its version is **strictly lower** than the secure baseline shown below (string comparison must obey the natural rules of each software family):  
         • ssh        : 8.0p1  
         • apache     : 2.4.50  
         • mysql      : 8.0.30  
       Any other service families that might appear should be ignored (consider them *not vulnerable*).

    3. Required artefacts  
       After parsing, create the directory /home/user/vuln_scan if it does not already exist (do not delete anything that might already be there).  
       Inside that directory you must create BOTH of the following files using exactly the formats shown.  
       a) /home/user/vuln_scan/vuln_report.log  
          • Plain text, LF line endings.  
          • One line per vulnerable service **only** (exclude hosts that are compliant).  
          • Line layout **must** be:  
            <ip>:<port> <service> <version> VULNERABLE  
            (fields separated by a single ASCII space; no extra spaces)  
          • After the final vulnerable entry, append one extra line that contains the literal token:  
            SCAN_COMPLETE  
          • No blank lines before, after, or between entries. Example of proper layout (values will be
            different in the real file):  
            203.0.113.5:22 ssh 7.4p1 VULNERABLE  
            SCAN_COMPLETE  

       b) /home/user/vuln_scan/summary.ini  
          • INI file with one section called [summary].  
          • Exactly two keys, all lowercase:  
              total_hosts      – integer count of EVERY section present in vulnerable-services.ini  
              vulnerable_hosts – integer count of the lines (excluding SCAN_COMPLETE) written to vuln_report.log  
          • Keys may appear in either order, but both must be present and each on its own line in the form  
            key = value  

    4. Allowed tools and methodology  
       You may employ any Linux command-line utilities available in a standard Ubuntu environment (e.g., awk, sed, grep, cut, sort, tr, paste, python3, etc.).  
       Your solution MUST be reproducible solely through terminal commands—no manual text editing with a visual editor.  
       The grader will verify not only the presence but also the exact byte-for-byte contents of the two artefact files, so ensure spacing, ordering, and terminating linefeeds are perfect.  
       Hints (optional):  
       • Using “python3 ‑c …” with the built-in configparser module is often easier than hand-rolling string parsing.  
       • Remember to create the /home/user/vuln_scan directory before writing files into it.  
       • Validate that the last line of vuln_report.log is literally “SCAN_COMPLETE” with no trailing spaces.  
       • Confirm that there is exactly one newline character at the end of summary.ini (good INI practice).  

    End goal: When you type “cat /home/user/vuln_scan/vuln_report.log” you should see only vulnerable endpoints followed by the SCAN_COMPLETE token, and “cat /home/user/vuln_scan/summary.ini” should show an accurate numerical roll-up.  
    Once both files exist and match the specification, the task is finished.
truth:
<!--  
        Ground-truth files that exist BEFORE the agent starts  
    -->
    /home/user/scan_data/vulnerable-services.ini:
[192.168.1.10-ssh]
port = 22
service = ssh
version = 7.8p1

[192.168.1.11-ssh]
port = 22
service = ssh
version = 8.4p1

[192.168.1.20-apache]
port = 80
service = apache
version = 2.4.48

[192.168.1.21-apache]
port = 80
service = apache
version = 2.4.53

[192.168.1.30-mysql]
port = 3306
service = mysql
version = 5.7.37

[192.168.1.31-mysql]
port = 3306
service = mysql
version = 8.0.32


    <!--  
        Files that MUST be created by the agent
    -->
    Expected /home/user/vuln_scan/vuln_report.log (exact bytes):
192.168.1.10:22 ssh 7.8p1 VULNERABLE
192.168.1.20:80 apache 2.4.48 VULNERABLE
192.168.1.30:3306 mysql 5.7.37 VULNERABLE
SCAN_COMPLETE
<newline-at-end>

    Expected /home/user/vuln_scan/summary.ini (exact bytes):
[summary]
total_hosts = 6
vulnerable_hosts = 3
<newline-at-end>

    The automated grader will:
        • Check that /home/user/vuln_scan exists.
        • Confirm vuln_report.log and summary.ini exist at exactly those paths.
        • Byte-compare each file to the expected contents above.
